environment.note: S.A.K. APFS containers attached as bare raw SATA disks (no GPT); macOS auto-synthesized and auto-mounted each container read-write at boot.

method: 1. S.A.K. APFS writer (userspace, no kernel driver) emitted each container image on Windows.
2. Images attached to qemu/KVM macOS Sequoia recovery as raw SATA disks.
3. macOS apfs.kext auto-mounted each container read-write (proven by 'last modified by apfs_kext (2332.101.1)' in the volume superblock).
4. diskutil unmountDisk force <disk>; fsck_apfs /dev/r<disk> run on each container.
5. Kernel read-write round-trip: diskutil mountDisk; mkdir + touch on the mounted volume; sync; unmount; fsck_apfs again. Checkpoint transaction id advanced and fsck still reports container OK.

fsck_apfs.verdict: The container /dev/rdisk0 appears to be OK.
fsck_apfs.volume_verdict: The volume /dev/rdisk0s1 appears to be OK.

kernel_write_roundtrip.post_write_fsck: The container /dev/rdisk0 appears to be OK.

fsck_apfs.verdict: The container /dev/rdisk1 appears to be OK.
fsck_apfs.volume_verdict: The volume /dev/rdisk1s1 appears to be OK.

kernel_write_roundtrip.post_write_fsck: The container /dev/rdisk1 appears to be OK.

fsck_apfs.verdict: The container /dev/rdisk2 appears to be OK.
fsck_apfs.volume_verdict: The volume /dev/rdisk2s1 appears to be OK.

kernel_write_roundtrip.note: fsck_apfs + kernel mount certified; explicit file round-trip performed on single-chunk and 256 MiB multi-chunk representatives.

production_recert.reason: The first run used the deterministic-UUID build. The writer was then changed to emit a cryptographically-random v4 UUID per container/volume at format and to read the on-disk UUID back during mutation (no deterministic fallback remains). The shipping output was re-certified.

results.fsck_apfs: The container /dev/rdisk0 appears to be OK.
The container /dev/rdisk1 appears to be OK.
The container /dev/rdisk3 appears to be OK.

production_recert.uniqueness_proof: Two same-size (64 MiB) same-name (TESTU) containers produced distinct v4 container UUIDs (ecfc9a1a-07ea-4077-... vs 9c508bc5-345f-4b12-...); a mutation round-trip preserved both the on-disk container and volume UUID byte-for-byte.

method: 1. Apple newfs_hfs created a 1 GiB non-journaled HFS+ volume 'SAKHFS' on a bare SATA disk in the macOS Sequoia recovery VM; baseline fsck_hfs reported the volume OK.

2. The image was detached and sak_hfs_writer_cli.exe (userspace, no kernel driver) ran create-file-image, adding /sak-cert.txt (54 bytes) with a known payload; ok=true, 'HFS+ alternate volume header synchronized'.
3. The mutated image was reattached; macOS auto-mounted it at /Volumes/SAKHFS and `cat` returned the exact payload (kernel HFS+ driver read the S.A.K.-written file byte-for-byte).
4. fsck_hfs -f on the unmounted volume reported 'The volume SAKHFS appears to be OK'.
hfs_cert.fsck_hfs: The volume SAKHFS appears to be OK.
follow_ups: RESOLVED 2026-06-14: the HFS writer now stamps catalog create/contentMod/attributeMod/access dates with the real time and HFSPlusBSDInfo owner=0 group=0 fileMode=0o100644 (files) / 0o040755 (folders). Byte-level verification of a newly created record showed createDate=2026-06-14 07:43:25, owner=0, group=0, mode=0x81a4. Gates (clang-format/lizard/cppcheck) and the full test suite pass.

method: 1. macOS mounted the S.A.K. container read-write; `mkfile 900m /Volumes/SAK8/crashtest` started a sustained write.
2. qemu was hard-killed (pkill -9) ~2 s into the write to simulate power loss mid-write.
3. The VM was rebooted into recovery.
4. macOS auto-mounted SAK8 again (APFS checkpoint recovery succeeded), and fsck_apfs on the unmounted container reported it OK.
crash_rollback_cert.post_crash_auto_mount: SAK8 re-mounted at /Volumes/SAK8 (APFS Container disk6, Physical Store disk0)
crash_rollback_cert.fsck_apfs: The container /dev/rdisk0 appears to be OK.
crash_rollback_cert.interpretation: The uncommitted mid-write data rolled back to the last valid checkpoint; no corruption. The S.A.K.-written container participates correctly in APFS copy-on-write checkpoint atomicity.

usb_destructive_cert.media: physical USB - JMicron Tech 238.5 GB (Windows Disk 3, disposable, user-authorized destruction)
method: 1. A 128 MiB S.A.K. APFS container (volume SAKUSB, random v4 UUID) was generated and written to the disposable JMicron disk's 128 MiB Apple_APFS GPT partition (Windows Disk 3, Partition 2).
2. The whole physical disk was attached to WSL2 via `wsl --mount --bare \.\PHYSICALDRIVE3` (device /dev/sde) and passed to the macOS Sequoia recovery VM as a raw SATA disk.
3. macOS recognized the partition as 'Apple_APFS Container disk3' on physical /dev/disk2s2 and auto-mounted volume SAKUSB read-write (superblock records last modified by apfs_kext (2332.101.1)).
4. fsck_apfs on the unmounted physical partition reported the container OK.
usb_destructive_cert.fsck_apfs: The container /dev/rdisk2s2 appears to be OK.
usb_destructive_cert.cleanup_note: Disk 3 remains attached to WSL2 (wsl --mount). Run elevated `wsl --unmount \.\PHYSICALDRIVE3` to return it to Windows.

evidence_chain_complete.apple_fsck_apfs: Passed (virtual SATA: SAK1/SAK2CHUNK/SAK8; physical USB: SAKUSB)
evidence_chain_complete.rollback: Passed (uncommitted write rolled back to last checkpoint; fsck OK)
evidence_chain_complete.note: All evidence required by the locked scope for an APFS raw-write matrix flip is now collected on Apple's own tools. Flip the certification matrix only after a human review of this report and the paired evidence screendumps.

follow_ups: Optional: extend physical-USB Apple-native validation to a multi-chunk (>128 MiB) partition; multi-chunk is already Apple-validated on virtual media.
Optional: re-run the HFS VM round-trip to visually confirm the new root:wheel owner and real mtime in Finder/ls (byte-level verification already confirms the catalog fields).
